back: Serializability is a property of a schedule where concurrent execution of multiple transactions produces the same result as some serial execution of those transactions, ensuring database consistency.
A serial schedule is one where transactions execute one after another without interleaving operations. No transaction starts until the previous one completes.

front: What are Conflict Operations in Serializability?
back: Two operations conflict if they belong to different transactions, operate on the same data item, and at least one is a write operation. Types: Read-Write, Write-Read, Write-Write.
A schedule is conflict serializable if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping adjacent non-conflicting operations. It focuses on conflict equivalence.

front: What is a Precedence Graph (Conflict Graph)?
back: A directed graph where nodes represent transactions and edges represent conflicts. An edge Ti → Tj exists if Tj reads/writes a data item written by Ti.

front: How do you test for Conflict Serializability?
back: Construct a precedence graph and check for cycles. If the graph is acyclic (no cycles), the schedule is conflict serializable. The topological order gives the equivalent serial schedule.
A schedule is view serializable if it is view-equivalent to some serial schedule. It considers the initial read, final write, and read-from relationships between transactions.

front: What is the difference between Conflict and View Serializability?
back: Conflict serializability is a subset of view serializability. All conflict-serializable schedules are view-serializable, but some view-serializable schedules are not conflict-serializable (contain blind writes).
A schedule is recoverable if a transaction commits only after all transactions whose data it has read have committed. Prevents dirty read problems.
A schedule where a transaction reads only data written by committed transactions. Prevents cascading rollbacks. All cascadeless schedules are recoverable.
A schedule where a transaction reads or writes data only after the transaction that wrote that data has committed. Prevents dirty reads and writes. All strict schedules are cascadeless.
Blind writes occur when a transaction writes a data item without first reading it. They help achieve view serializability but make conflict serializability testing more complex.
Two schedules are conflict-equivalent if they contain the same operations and order of conflicting operations is the same. Conflicting operations must maintain the same relative order.
Two schedules are view-equivalent if: (1) Same initial reads, (2) Same read-from relationships, (3) Same final writes. Used for view serializability testing.

front: What is a Polygraph in Serializability?
back: The polygraph is used to test view serializability. It extends the precedence graph with additional edges to capture read-from relationships and is more complex than precedence graph testing.
